text: The surgeon begins the first step of the gastrectomy resection after visually confirming the absence of distant metastases on the liver and peritoneal surface.
The surgeon performs an omentectomy, carefully resecting the omental tissue from the surrounding structures.

The surgeon carefully dissects the thin rim of tissue between the third layer of the omentum and the transverse mesocolon, ensuring precise separation to avoid damage to the mesocolon and its vessels.
In the video, the surgeon observes a clear demarcation between the yellow transverse mesocolon and the white third layer of the omentum in a relatively thin patient.
This is an important step in the initial dissection.

The surgeon approaches the head of the pancreas, where the gastroepiploic vein converges with the superior mesenteric vein and the loop of Henle. Gentle dissection is performed to avoid bleeding, which could complicate future steps.
The surgeon carefully bares the right gastroepiploic vein of lymphatic tissue and clips it at its root, ensuring clear visualization of the vein's structure.
The surgeon bares the right gastroepiploic artery of lymphatic tissue and clips it at its root, similar to the previous treatment of the gastroepiploic vein.
The surgeon dissects the final bit of tissue between the pedicle and the pylorus, ensuring all soft tissue remains with the specimen.
The surgeon incises the lesser omentum, maintaining an adequate margin from the stomach to ensure proper separation of the structures.
The surgeon coagulates the right gastric artery at its root, ensuring all surrounding lymphatic tissue is directed towards the specimen.
The surgeon has separated the gastroduodenal junction from surrounding soft tissues. The division of the lesser omentum is extended superiorly towards the diaphragm.

The surgeon dissects the left gastric vein, removing all surrounding soft tissue from the crux of the diaphragm and ensuring it remains with the specimen, due to the proximal gastric tumor involving the fundus and body.
The surgeon prepares to divide the stomach at its junction with the first part of the duodenum, encountering difficulty in further dissection.
The surgeon bares the left gastric artery of all lymphatic tissue, pushing it towards the specimen. The origin of the left gastric artery is identified, and a clip is applied.

The surgeon has ligated the left gastric artery and vein and is now addressing adhesions between the stomach and the pancreas. Using a ligature, the surgeon carefully separates the flimsy adhesions between the gastric tumor and the pancreatic capsule, ensuring no tumor tissue remains on the pancreas while avoiding injury to the pancreatic tissue.
Many times we have seen that after open gastrectomy, patients do develop some degree of pancreatitis because of the handling. Minimally invasive surgery definitely holds an upper hand in this part.
The surgeon divides the remaining adhesions between the stomach and the diaphragm's crura, ensuring that all tissue affected by neoadjuvant chemotherapy is included with the specimen.

The surgeon divides the short gastric vessels from the medial side of the stomach, demonstrating careful dissection along the greater curvature. The anatomical proximity to the spleen is visible, highlighting the need for precision to avoid injury and prevent major bleeding.

A gauze piece is placed at the hilum of the spleen to maintain dryness. The surgeon completes the mobilization of the entire stomach, ensuring a substantial amount of surrounding soft tissue is included and the vessels are taken at their root.
The surgeon proceeds to remove lymph nodes situated along the common hepatic artery, extending up to the right side of the right hepatic artery, following the complete mobilization of the stomach.
